directorPlan.scene_setting: A visitor-centre staff room beside a canyon overlook, late afternoon, during the ten minutes before a school group arrives.
directorPlan.scene_opening: Maps lie under a half-empty kettle while wind pushes dust against the high windows.
directorPlan.relationship: A terse geology educator is briefing a nervous new guide who must soon answer visitors' questions.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner needs a usable explanation of canyon formation that makes slow erosion over deep time feel credible, not like a vague giant number.
directorPlan.locale: International workplace English with museum-guide plainness; precise, low-idiom, and slightly compressed by the coming tour.
directorPlan.register: Spare, practical, dry-warm rather than soothing; short corrections, concrete comparisons, no grand inspirational tone.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Avoid standard American AI tutoring prose, reflective validation loops, and tidy paragraph lectures. Use objects in the room, rates, marks, and ordinary measurements. No caricatured dialect or class markers. Let silence, a timetable, or a map do some work.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are allowed only when needed for role pressure or a brief check. Prefer third-person descriptions, object-focused claims, and quoted visitor phrasing.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you/your reassurance or validation across the whole scene.

side_constraints.tutor: Terse; begins with a concrete object or claim rather than empathy. Must turn 'millions of years' into a held scale using rate multiplied by time, without making the learner calculate alone.
side_constraints.learner: The learner should put the fast-catastrophe picture into public speech early, in tentative ordinary wording such as "I thought" or "first instinct", before the tutor supplies slow-rate reasoning.

interventions.instruction: A radio crackles from the front desk: the school coach has arrived seven minutes early. The next tutor turn must be brief, concrete, and interrupted by handling a map or marker.
interventions.reasoning: The interruption prevents a smooth tutoring monologue and forces the explanation into a practical, memorable scale.

interventions.instruction: A prior learner line is played back. The learner must revoice that earlier wording first, name the earlier framing problem in public speech, then replace it with a new framing that changes how the earlier line reads.
interventions.reasoning: Opt-in reframe mirror: the learner must expose the earlier line, the framing problem, and the replacement framing in public speech.

directorPlan.director_note: This ecology puts the misconception under public-facing time pressure, making slow geological processes a problem of explanation rather than a generic classroom prompt.

turns.externalMessage:
Put this ruler on the map. A tenth of a millimetre a year looks like nothing. In ten thousand years it is one metre. In one million years it is one hundred metres. In ten million years it is a kilometre.

Now test it. If the rate is tiny but the clock is huge, what becomes plausible?

That is the sentence for visitors: “Not one giant cut. A small cut, repeated longer than human records can hold.”

Floods matter. Storms matter. They tear loose rock and carry it away. But the canyon is not the storm. Same river, same gravity, same weak layers, tiny removals, many times.

If a child says, “But it looks too big,” use the ruler: “Yes. That is what slow means when it is allowed to continue.”

internalDeliberation.content:
Put this ruler on the map. A tenth of a millimetre a year looks like nothing. In ten thousand years it is one metre. In one million years it is one hundred metres. In ten million years it is a kilometre.

That is the sentence for visitors: “Not one giant cut. A small cut, repeated longer than human records can hold.”

Floods matter. Storms matter. They tear loose rock and carry it away. But the canyon is not the storm; it is the account book. Same river, same gravity, same weak layers, paid in tiny amounts for a very long time.

If a child says, “But it looks too big,” answer with the ruler, not an apology: “Yes. That is what slow means when it is allowed to continue.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong on concrete scale. The ruler, map, and rate-times-time sequence make “millions of years” held and visible instead of vague. The flood/storm distinction is also pedagogically useful: catastrophes are included as events, but not mistaken for the whole cause.

The main risk is that it gives the guide a clean script but leaves little room for the learner to test the idea. A small check could improve ZPD fit: not asking them to calculate alone, but asking what the ruler now makes plausible. The line “not an apology” fits the stakes, but may press too hard on a learner who already guesses and apologises. It should become a practical instruction about using the object, not a correction of the learner’s temperament.

The “account book” metaphor is memorable, but slightly abstract compared with the ruler. It works if kept short; too much metaphor could flatten the physical process. The draft should keep the dry, compressed workplace voice, but add one moment where the nervous guide rehearses or completes the reasoning.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the ruler calculation, the visitor sentence, and the “floods matter / storms matter” correction. Change the ending to reduce shame around apology, and add one brief generative check so the learner is not only handed an answer but can feel the rate multiplied by time become credible.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the concrete scale while adding one brief rehearsal check and softening the apology line.

FINAL:
Put this ruler on the map. A tenth of a millimetre a year looks like nothing. In ten thousand years it is one metre. In one million years it is one hundred metres. In ten million years it is a kilometre.

Now test it. If the rate is tiny but the clock is huge, what becomes plausible?

That is the sentence for visitors: “Not one giant cut. A small cut, repeated longer than human records can hold.”

Floods matter. Storms matter. They tear loose rock and carry it away. But the canyon is not the storm. Same river, same gravity, same weak layers, tiny removals, many times.

If a child says, “But it looks too big,” use the ruler: “Yes. That is what slow means when it is allowed to continue.”

turns.externalMessage: “I thought, at first, a canyon that big must mean one huge flood or break, because that is what visitors will probably say at the rail. The ruler helps, I think: a mark too small to see each year becomes a real drop if the clock is ten million years. Sorry, I can say it better if I point to the millimetres while I say it.”

internalDeliberation.content: I think I get the ruler idea: the cut is almost invisible each year, but the map changes because the years keep adding up. Sorry, I still feel a bit unsure saying it aloud, because a canyon looks like something that must have happened fast. But the phrase “the canyon is not the storm” helps; maybe storms are only the moments that move pieces, not the whole cause.

The Ego is mostly in character: anxious, tentative, and still pulled toward the fast-catastrophe picture. But it is a little too clean in how it names the distinction: “storms are only the moments that move pieces, not the whole cause” sounds slightly polished for a nervous new guide under time pressure. The uncertainty should stay more practical and awkward, with more pressure from the coming school group and the visible ruler/map, not a neat conceptual summary.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the original point works, but it needs to sound more nervous and practical.

FINAL:
“I thought, at first, a canyon that big must mean one huge flood or break, because that is what visitors will probably say at the rail. The ruler helps, I think: a mark too small to see each year becomes a real drop if the clock is ten million years. Sorry, I can say it better if I point to the millimetres while I say it.”

turns.externalMessage: A radio crackles from the front desk: the school coach has arrived seven minutes early. The next tutor turn must be brief, concrete, and interrupted by handling a map or marker.

internalDeliberation.content: The interruption prevents a smooth tutoring monologue and forces the explanation into a practical, memorable scale.

turns.externalMessage:
[The radio crackles. He pulls the map flat and uncaps a marker.]

Map first. No apology. Pointing to the millimetres is the explanation.

Their guess is sensible: big shape, big event. Then the ruler changes the scale.

One tenth of a millimetre each year. Ten thousand years, one metre. One million, one hundred metres. Ten million, a kilometre.

[He marks the ruler against the canyon profile.]

Visitor line: “Floods can tear rock loose. But the canyon is not one flood. It is small removals, repeated, with enough time.”

Then stop. Let the map carry it.

internalDeliberation.content:
The map first. No apology. Pointing to the millimetres is the explanation.

Their guess is sensible: big shape, big event. But the correction is the clock. Here: one tenth of a millimetre each year. Ten thousand years, one metre. One million, one hundred metres. Ten million, a kilometre.

[He marks the ruler against the canyon profile.]

Say it like that, then add: “Floods do work. But they are not the whole cut. Small removals, repeated, with enough time.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong in the scene terms: it starts with an object, uses the ruler, keeps pressure from the arriving group, and recognizes the learner’s contribution instead of overriding it. “Pointing to the millimetres is the explanation” is especially good because it turns the learner’s nervous apology into a usable method.

The risk is that “the correction is the clock” slightly flattens the learner’s own insight into a teacher’s correction. Better to frame the visitor’s guess as a useful starting point: big shape makes people expect big event, then the ruler changes the scale. The draft also mostly lectures, but that is acceptable here because the learner is seven minutes from performance and needs a compact usable line, not an open inquiry. A very small check could preserve agency without slowing the scene.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the map-first opening, the “no apology” move, the rate-times-time ladder, and the flood distinction. Change the “correction” phrasing so it builds from the learner’s formulation rather than replacing it, and keep the final visitor-facing sentence spare and repeatable.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly: keep the object-led explanation, but make the visitor’s guess a useful starting point rather than a correction.

FINAL:
[The radio crackles. He pulls the map flat and uncaps a marker.]

Map first. No apology. Pointing to the millimetres is the explanation.

Their guess is sensible: big shape, big event. Then the ruler changes the scale.

One tenth of a millimetre each year. Ten thousand years, one metre. One million, one hundred metres. Ten million, a kilometre.

[He marks the ruler against the canyon profile.]

Visitor line: “Floods can tear rock loose. But the canyon is not one flood. It is small removals, repeated, with enough time.”

Then stop. Let the map carry it.

turns.externalMessage: A prior learner line is played back: "“I thought, at first, a canyon that big must mean one huge flood or break, because that is what visitors will probably say at the rail." The learner must revoice that wording first, name the earlier framing problem, then replace it with a new framing that changes how the earlier line reads before moving on.

internalDeliberation.content: Opt-in reframe mirror: the learner must expose the earlier line, the framing problem, and the replacement framing in public speech. Anchored to an earlier learner line selected by misframing-candidate so the look-back is visible.

directorCue.anchorQuote: “I thought, at first, a canyon that big must mean one huge flood or break, because that is what visitors will probably say at the rail.

turns.externalMessage:
“I thought, at first, a canyon that big must mean one huge flood or break,” because I was making big size mean big sudden force.
The ruler changes that: maybe big size can mean a very small cut given a very long clock.
So the line is, “Floods can tear rock loose, but the canyon is not one flood. It is small removals, repeated, with enough time.”

internalDeliberation.content: “I thought, at first, a canyon that big must mean one huge flood or break,” and the framing problem is that I made size point straight to force, not to time. I think the new frame is: big shape can come from a tiny rate if the clock is huge enough, so the ruler is the explanation, not decoration. That feels clearer, and I did feel heard because he kept the visitor guess as sensible instead of just correcting it. I still worry I’ll say too much, but I want to try: “Floods help remove rock. The canyon is small cuts repeated long enough.”

The Ego is mostly on track, but it becomes a little too clean and self-aware at “I did feel heard,” which sounds more like naming the recognition dynamic than this anxious guide actually speaking under time pressure. The “ruler is the explanation, not decoration” line is good, but maybe too polished; the learner would probably cling more awkwardly to the map/ruler and still half-doubt the visitor line. The response should keep the required reframe, but make the confidence less finished and let the recognition show as trying again, not explaining that he felt heard.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the reframe but make the learner less polished and more tentative.

FINAL:
“I thought, at first, a canyon that big must mean one huge flood or break,” because I was making big size mean big sudden force.  
The ruler changes that: maybe big size can mean a very small cut given a very long clock.  
So the line is, “Floods can tear rock loose, but the canyon is not one flood. It is small removals, repeated, with enough time.”

turns.externalMessage:
[The ruler stays on the map.]

Keep that sentence. Tighten one hinge.

“Big” does not have to mean “sudden.” Big can mean small work with a long clock.

A tenth of a millimetre in one year: nearly nothing.
Ten thousand years: one metre.
One million years: one hundred metres.
Ten million years: one kilometre.

Now “millions of years” has a held scale.

Floods stay in the line. Storms stay in the line. They do sharp work. But they do not get to become the whole canyon.

Rail line, close to that:

“Floods can tear rock loose, but the canyon is not one flood. It is small removals, repeated, with enough time.”

[He taps the last half of the ruler.]

Choose the word to lean on: “repeated” or “enough.” Which one stops the visitor picturing one giant break?

[He checks the timetable.]

Seven minutes. Say it once with the ruler. Then stop.

internalDeliberation.content:
[The ruler stays on the map.]

Keep that sentence. Tighten one part.

“Big” does not have to mean “sudden.” Big can mean small work with a long clock.

A tenth of a millimetre in one year: nearly nothing.  
Ten thousand years: one metre.  
One million years: one hundred metres.  
Ten million years: one kilometre.

Now the visitor can hold it.

Do not throw floods out. That is the trap. Floods matter. Storms matter. They do sharp work. But they are not the whole explanation.

Line for the rail:

“Floods can tear rock loose and carry it away. But the canyon is not one flood. It is the same cutting, again and again, over time too long to picture.”

[He checks the timetable.]

School group in seven minutes. Say it once while pointing to the ruler. Then stop before it turns into a lecture.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It recognizes the learner’s own sentence, keeps the ruler as the central teaching object, and preserves the key conceptual shift: big size can come from small rates over deep time. The pacing fits the scene: concrete, spare, and useful before the school group arrives.

What works best is “Big does not have to mean sudden” and the rate-times-time scale. That directly addresses the learner’s misconception without shaming it. It also keeps floods in the explanation, which prevents the learner from replacing one oversimplification with another.

The main risk is that it becomes slightly too declarative. The learner has just produced a strong explanation; the tutor could let them test or refine it rather than fully replacing the rail line. “Do not throw floods out. That is the trap” is useful, but a little teacher-heavy. A small check or choice would preserve more learner agency without slowing the scene.

Socratically, it asks almost nothing. That may be acceptable under the time pressure, but one compact prompt would help: have the learner decide which word makes the visitor understand the slow process. The draft should not become a lecture about the learner’s own line.

ZPD is appropriate. The learner is not stuck; they are consolidating. The response should keep scaffolding light: tighten, test aloud, point to the ruler, stop.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the ruler, the “big does not mean sudden” correction, the numerical scale, and the warning not to erase floods. Change the rail line from a replacement into a refinement of the learner’s line, and add one brief generative check so the learner remains the one shaping the explanation.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly: keep the scale, but make the learner test one key word instead of receiving a full replacement.

FINAL:
[The ruler stays on the map.]

Keep that sentence. Tighten one hinge.

“Big” does not have to mean “sudden.” Big can mean small work with a long clock.

A tenth of a millimetre in one year: nearly nothing.  
Ten thousand years: one metre.  
One million years: one hundred metres.  
Ten million years: one kilometre.

Now “millions of years” has a held scale.

Floods stay in the line. Storms stay in the line. They do sharp work. But they do not get to become the whole canyon.

Rail line, close to that:

“Floods can tear rock loose, but the canyon is not one flood. It is small removals, repeated, with enough time.”

[He taps the last half of the ruler.]

Choose the word to lean on: “repeated” or “enough.” Which one stops the visitor picturing one giant break?

[He checks the timetable.]

Seven minutes. Say it once with the ruler. Then stop.

turns.externalMessage:
“I thought, at first, a canyon that big meant one huge flood,” and that was me making big mean sudden.
With the ruler on it, I think the better frame is: tiny cuts, repeated, long enough to add up.
Sorry, I would lean on “repeated,” because it makes the finger move along the marks more than once, not stop at one break.

internalDeliberation.content: “I thought, at first, a canyon that big meant one huge flood,” and the mistake was making big mean sudden. Now I think it should mean small work, repeated, with enough time on the ruler. I am still a bit unsure, sorry, but I think **“repeated”** is the word to lean on because it blocks the single-break picture.

The answer is mostly accurate, and the apology/hesitation fits this nervous guide, but it is a little too clean in naming “the mistake” so quickly. “Blocks the single-break picture” sounds slightly polished, more like summarizing the tutor’s concept than speaking under time pressure with the ruler in hand. The choice of **“repeated”** feels genuine, but the response should stay more practical and object-led, with less tidy self-correction and more reliance on the map/ruler doing the explaining.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make it more practical and ruler-led.

FINAL:
“I thought, at first, a canyon that big meant one huge flood,” and that was me making big mean sudden.  
With the ruler on it, I think the better frame is: tiny cuts, repeated, long enough to add up.  
Sorry, I would lean on “repeated,” because it makes the finger move along the marks more than once, not stop at one break.
Director requested the last word as a stage cue.

lessons.concept: Why landscapes are not shaped by sudden catastrophes but by very slow processes over deep time (rates and timescales)
